# Navigating Amazon's Authenticity Concerns Without Customer Complaints: Seeking Guidance and Resolution

I wanted to bring everyone's attention to an issue I'm facing and seek your advice on whether I'm right or wrong here.

A few days ago, I received an email from Amazon regarding the authenticity of an item I've been selling for the past month through FBA. Despite not receiving any negative feedback or complaints from customers during this time, Amazon has raised concerns. [...] It's frustrating dealing with a company as large as Amazon, which seems unable to efficiently verify a single invoice despite being a trillion-dollar entity.

user profile

That will not help you. It's useless if YOU are sure that the products are authentic. You have to convince Amazon that they are. And that is done ONLY by providing:

1. A "Letter of Authorization" from the brand allowing you to sell their brand on Amazon. [...] At this point, my only recourse seems to be physically verifying the authenticity of the inventory at the warehouse where it's stored. I plan to purchase the same item directly from the manufacturer's dealer, pick it up from their warehouse, and compare it with the inventory in question. It's an extreme step, but given the lack of progress with Amazon's invoice verification process, it seems necessary.

It's frustrating dealing with a company as large as Amazon, which seems unable to efficiently verify a single invoice despite being a trillion-dollar entity.

results.title: Amazon is rated "Bad" with 1.6 / 5 on Trustpilot
results.content:
Considering 5,642 reviews, most reviewers were let down by their experience overall. Many customers reported significant dissatisfaction with delivery services, citing frequent delays, missed delivery dates, and items not arriving as expected. Reviewers also expressed frustration with customer service, describing it as unhelpful, difficult to reach, and often providing broken promises or inadequat e solutions. Additionally, many people encountered issues with their orders, including incorrect items being sent, damaged products upon arrival, or parts of an order missing. However, some customers also noted positive experiences with delivery speed and product quality, particularly for specific items, and found the customer service to be satisfactory in some instances.See more [...] your company into Frankenstein's monster. Fix your crap or go out of business and give someone else a chance to do better. [...] ### The Trustpilot Experience

#### We're open to all

Anyone can write a Trustpilot review. People who write reviews have ownership to edit or delete them at any time, and they’ll be displayed as long as an account is active.

#### We champion verified reviews

Companies can ask for reviews via automatic invitations. Labeled Verified, they’re about genuine experiences.

Learn more about other kinds of reviews.

results.title: How to Avoid Scams and Bad Gadgets on Amazon (2026) | WIRED
results.content: You can also browse the reviews yourself. Click on the link to reviews under the product name on a page and you'll end up on a dedicated reviews section. To start, browse through the customer questions. You can use the search box above them to scan questions and reviews for keywords that may indicate issues with a product, like "break,” “bad,” “defective,” “customer service,” or "return." Amazon has also implemented an AI-generated summary of reviews on most product pages, but take these with a grain of salt. For example, this blender's AI-generated summary says that customers like the quality and durability of the blender, but that some customers find it to be quiet while others think it's too loud. That's not very useful information—you're better off reading the reviews on your own. [...] Check for the “frequently returned” flag: Sometimes a product page will show a flag that says it's a “frequently returned item.” The reasons for this aren't usually listed, so it's up to you to read through the reviews. Sometimes an item is frequently returned because it's available in several confusing options; sometimes it's a seller issue; sometimes the product breaks easily. A “frequently returned” flag isn't an immediate reason for dismissal, but if you see it, check out the critical and most recent reviews carefully. [...] It's remarkably easy to sell products on Amazon (as evinced by its millions of sellers). That means there are plenty of subpar product listings that come from sketchy sellers—often with gibberish names or multiple sellers that are using the same photos and claiming to sell the same product. While many items on Amazon are safe and legitimate, you do need to be aware of the risks when you're shopping. The site has problems with fake reviews and counterfeit items. Amazon has fairly good return policies, including an A-to-Z guarantee for items sold by third parties, but ideally you want to avoid having to deal with a return in the first place.

Hello @Seller\_5WnJwxlpJ0pFN,

user profile

I definitely hear your frustration and understand your concern. I would like to be able to offer guidance, but I believe you do understand the current process.

If you would like me to investigate anything for you, please provide an ASIN, Case ID or Performance Notification. With that information and help from an internal team, I have more ability to review your specifics.

I'm pretty sure you may have already reviewed the help information, but I will include it here as it may benefit others: [...] In my case, the buyer sent a message stating it was counterfeit and then immediately followed up with another message stating they believed they received the wrong color item and wanted to exchange it. But it still triggered the complaint and I have to spend all day satisfying the appeal process. There needs to be a better way. 365 days worth of invoices is not reasonable. Maybe it should start with 30/60/90 days worth of sales, and if after review it doesn't satisfy then request the full 365 days.

results.title: Welcome to the Era of Fake Products | Wirecutter
results.content:
Property Accelerator, a program to help smaller brands procure early intellectual property protection; and Utility Patent Neutral Evaluation, a program that helps owners of a patent get knockoffs removed from Amazon without going through a lengthy and expensive legal process. [...] Sumroy, the Mifold travel booster seat CEO, credits Amazon with at least trying to combat what has become an international problem. “My fear isn’t Amazon, my fear is the eBays and AliExpresses and whatever crops up next,” he says. Although the programs Amazon provides are more adept at stomping out copyright and trademark infringements than they are at getting rid of knockoffs, Sumroy has had success in taking down sellers who use his imagery or packaging. “We’ve been singularly unsuccessful with Ebay,” he says. “There are no real tools with Alibaba and AliExpress, not like Amazon is trying.”

### How to be smarter about fakes
